stem: Two abelian groups of the same order can fail to be isomorphic even when they look superficially similar; one reliable invariant that distinguishes them is the MAXIMUM order attained by any single element. What is the maximum order of an element in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Every element's order in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]}\times\mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) divides \( \operatorname{lcm}([[m]],[[n]]) \), and the element \( (1,1) \) attains exactly this order, so the maximum is \( \operatorname{lcm}([[m]],[[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the ORDER of the whole group, not the maximum element order.
This adds the two moduli instead of taking their LCM.
This is the GCD of the moduli, which is unrelated to the maximum element order.
This assumes the maximum order can never exceed either individual factor's order, ignoring that a pair can have larger order than each component.
This is off by one from the correct maximum order.
This is off by one from the correct maximum order.

stem: The number of elements of order exactly [[p]] is a useful invariant for telling non-isomorphic abelian groups of the same order apart. How many elements of order EXACTLY [[p]] does \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) have (both [[m]] and [[n]] are multiples of the prime [[p]])?
solution: Each factor \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \) and \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) (both multiples of [[p]]) contributes exactly [[p]] elements \( x \) with \( [[p]]\,x=0 \); pairing these gives \( [[p]]\cdot[[p]] \) elements of order dividing [[p]], and removing the identity leaves \( [[p]]^2-1 = [[answer]] \) elements of order exactly [[p]].
distractor_rationales: This only counts the order-[[p]] elements within a single cyclic factor, ignoring the other factor entirely.
This doubles the single-factor count instead of using the product-then-subtract-one rule.
This forgets to remove the identity element from the count.
This is an unrelated ratio of the group orders, not a count of order-[[p]] elements.
This overshoots the correct count by one.
This undershoots the correct count by one.

stem: The group \( G = \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) (with [[p]] prime) is NOT isomorphic to \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]^2} \), even though both have order \( [[p]]^2 \); one way to see this is to count subgroups of order [[p]] (a cyclic group of order \( [[p]]^2 \) has only one). How many subgroups of order [[p]] does \( G = \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) have?
solution: \( G \) has \( [[p]]^2-1 \) non-identity elements, each of order [[p]] since \( G \) is elementary abelian; each subgroup of order [[p]] contains \( [[p]]-1 \) of them and distinct subgroups of order [[p]] only meet at the identity, so there are \( \frac{[[p]]^2-1}{[[p]]-1} = [[p]]+1 = [[answer]] \) such subgroups.
distractor_rationales: This is the count for a CYCLIC group of order \( [[p]]^2 \), which has a unique subgroup per divisor; \( G \) is not cyclic, so it has more.
This restates [[p]] itself instead of counting subgroups.
This is the total number of non-identity elements, not the number of subgroups they fall into.
This forgets to divide by the number of non-identity elements per subgroup.
This overshoots the correct subgroup count by one.
This undershoots the correct subgroup count by one.
